content.text: What the Toolkit Is — and Is Not

A champion enablement toolkit is the pre-built resource set that allows a champion to respond to a colleague's question, run a peer session, or onboard a new team member without having to author content from scratch each time. It is not a generic AI marketing deck: every artefact is specific enough to be deployed immediately, and generic enough to be adapted across role families without rewriting.

The toolkit distinguishes champions from enthusiasts. Any motivated employee can read Claude's documentation; only a champion has curated, battle-tested, organisation-specific resources ready to deploy in a 15-minute slot between meetings.
Curated, role-tagged reusable prompts validated for quality
Turn-key session package: slides, agenda, demo script, follow-up email
Pre-answered objections and edge cases (data safety, accuracy, policy)
Role-specific before/after workflow blueprints with starter prompts
Decision tree: when to involve security, legal, or CoP council
Before/after case study format for capturing wins

The prompt library is the highest-leverage toolkit component. A well-curated library reduces time-to-first-value for a new Claude user from days to minutes. Champions contribute prompts; the CoP council validates quality before publication.
— each prompt is tagged to one or more role families (engineering, legal, HR, finance, product, data) so users can filter immediately to relevant content
— prompts that score ≥7/10 in the validation pipeline are marked 'approved'; lower scores are held in 'draft' pending revision
— what the user needs to provide (e.g. a transcript, a list of requirements, a contract) is stated explicitly at the top of each prompt
— a sample output from running the prompt helps users calibrate expectations before committing it to their workflow

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json
from pathlib import Path

client = Anthropic()

def add_prompt_to_library(
    title: str,
    role_tag: str,
    use_case: str,
    prompt_template: str,
    example_input: str
) -> dict:
    """Validate and enrich a prompt before adding it to the shared library."""
    validation_prompt = f"""You are a senior Claude practitioner reviewing a prompt template for an enterprise prompt library.

Prompt title: {title}
Target role: {role_tag}
Use case: {use_case}

Prompt template:
---
{prompt_template}
---

Example input to test with:
---
{example_input}
---

Evaluate this prompt and return JSON with:
- quality_score: integer 1-10
- strengths: list of 2-3 specific strengths
- improvements: list of 0-3 specific improvements (empty list if none needed)
- improved_template: the template with improvements applied (or original if quality_score >= 8)
- one_line_description: a 15-word max description for the library index
- tags: list of 3-5 searchable tags"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=800,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": validation_prompt}]
    )
    evaluation = json.loads(response.content[0].text)

    entry = {
        "title": title,
        "role_tag": role_tag,
        "use_case": use_case,
        "template": evaluation["improved_template"],
        "description": evaluation["one_line_description"],
        "tags": evaluation["tags"],
        "quality_score": evaluation["quality_score"],
        "contributed_by": "champion",
        "status": "approved" if evaluation["quality_score"] >= 7 else "needs_revision"
    }
    return entry

entry = add_prompt_to_library(
    title="Sprint Retrospective Summariser",
    role_tag="engineering",
    use_case="Summarise a raw retrospective transcript into structured went-well / delta / actions",
    prompt_template="""Summarise this sprint retrospective transcript.

Transcript:
{{transcript}}

Output:
- What went well (3-5 bullet points)
- What to improve (3-5 bullet points)
- Action items with owner and due date""",
    example_input="Team velocity was good. The new CI pipeline broke twice. John will fix the flaky tests by next Friday. We liked the async standup format."
)
print(f"Status: {entry['status']} (score: {entry['quality_score']}/10)")
print(f"Description: {entry['description']}")
print(f"Tags: {', '.join(entry['tags'])}")

A structured peer session is the highest-adoption driver in the champion toolkit. Done well, a 45-minute lunch-and-learn converts 30–60% of attendees into active users within two weeks. Done poorly — generic slides, no live demo, no hands-on segment — it confirms sceptics' suspicion that AI is hype.

Every lunch-and-learn must include a live demo with a real example from the audience's own domain, and a hands-on segment where attendees try a prompt themselves during the session. These two elements are non-negotiable.

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def generate_lunch_and_learn_agenda(
    team_name: str,
    team_function: str,
    audience_familiarity: str,
    duration_minutes: int,
    focus_use_case: str
) -> dict:
    """Generate a complete lunch-and-learn agenda tailored to the team."""
    prompt = f"""Create a {duration_minutes}-minute lunch-and-learn agenda for a Claude champion
to deliver to their team.

Team: {team_name} ({team_function} function)
Audience familiarity: {audience_familiarity}
Focus use case: {focus_use_case}

Design the session so it:
1. Opens with a hook that connects to a pain the audience already feels
2. Includes a live demo with a real example relevant to {team_function} work
3. Has a hands-on segment where attendees try a prompt themselves
4. Ends with clear next steps and an offer to follow up

Return JSON with:
- title: session title (catchy, outcome-focused)
- pre_work: what the champion should prepare (max 3 items)
- segments: list of {{name, duration_min, description, champion_notes}}
- demo_prompt: the exact prompt to use in the live demo
- hands_on_prompt: the prompt for attendees to try themselves
- follow_up_email: a 3-sentence follow-up email template to send same day"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=1200,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

agenda = generate_lunch_and_learn_agenda(
    team_name="Retail Analytics",
    team_function="data science and business intelligence",
    audience_familiarity="Has heard of Claude but never used it directly",
    duration_minutes=45,
    focus_use_case="Drafting SQL query explanations for non-technical stakeholders"
)
print(f"Title: {agenda['title']}")
print("\nSegments:")
for seg in agenda["segments"]:
    print(f"  [{seg['duration_min']} min] {seg['name']}: {seg['description']}")
print(f"\nDemo prompt: {agenda['demo_prompt'][:100]}...")

Champions encounter the same ten questions in almost every peer interaction. Building a curated, policy-grounded FAQ playbook means champions can respond consistently and accurately without improvising on data safety or compliance questions — where an off-the-cuff answer can cause real harm.
Most Common Questions (and How to Handle Them)
Data safety ('Can I paste customer data?')
Cite the organisation's data classification policy; explain the enterprise gateway's zero-retention guarantee; give the approved anonymisation step
Accuracy ('What if Claude gets it wrong?')
Explain the human-in-the-loop principle; give the 'spot check' habit; frame Claude as a first draft, not a final answer
Job security ('Will this replace my job?')
Reframe as leverage: Claude handles volume, humans handle judgment; share the time-saved data from early adopters in similar roles
State the current policy: approved use cases require no additional sign-off; novel use cases go through the champion for a quick policy check
Intellectual property ('Who owns what Claude writes?')
Share the organisation's IP policy; note the output is a starting point that requires human authorship to reach publishable quality

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def generate_faq_response(
    question: str,
    organisation_context: str,
    approved_policies: list[str]
) -> dict:
    """Generate a champion-ready FAQ response grounded in organisational policy."""
    policies_text = "\n".join(f"- {p}" for p in approved_policies)
    prompt = f"""A colleague has asked a Claude champion this question:
"{question}"

Organisation context: {organisation_context}

Approved policies and guardrails in place:
{policies_text}

Generate a champion-ready response that:
1. Directly answers the question in plain language
2. References specific policies where relevant (by name, not by making them up)
3. Gives a concrete example where it helps understanding
4. Notes any legitimate caveats without being alarmist
5. Ends with a clear action or next step

Return JSON with:
- headline_answer: one sentence (the TL;DR)
- full_response: 150-200 word conversational response
- example: a concrete illustration
- caveats: list of 0-2 specific caveats (empty if none)
- next_step: what the colleague should do now"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=700,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

answer = generate_faq_response(
    question="Can I paste customer data into Claude to help write a support response?",
    organisation_context="B2B SaaS company, Claude accessed via internal enterprise gateway with data retention disabled",
    approved_policies=[
        "Personal data must be anonymised before use in AI tools",
        "Enterprise gateway has zero retention — inputs are not stored after the session",
        "Customer names and email addresses count as personal data under our data policy",
        "Approval not required for support response drafting if data is anonymised"
    ]
)
print(f"TL;DR: {answer['headline_answer']}")
print(f"\nCaveats: {answer['caveats']}")
print(f"Next step: {answer['next_step']}")

Use-case playbooks are the most time-intensive toolkit component to author but the most persuasive for sceptical teams. A playbook shows the exact workflow, before and after Claude, with realistic time savings, the specific prompt that initiates the change, and a concrete signal of success. Vague claims ('Claude helps you work faster') persuade no one; a before/after workflow with '45 minutes saved per contract review' converts the sceptic.

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def generate_use_case_playbook(
    function: str,
    use_case_title: str,
    current_workflow_steps: list[str],
    pain_points: list[str]
) -> dict:
    """Generate a step-by-step use-case playbook for a specific team function."""
    steps_text = "\n".join(f"{i+1}. {s}" for i, s in enumerate(current_workflow_steps))
    pains_text = "\n".join(f"- {p}" for p in pain_points)
    prompt = f"""Create a practical use-case playbook for integrating Claude into a specific workflow.

Function: {function}
Use case: {use_case_title}

Current workflow (before Claude):
{steps_text}

Pain points:
{pains_text}

Design the Claude-augmented workflow and return JSON with:
- executive_summary: 2-sentence value proposition for a manager
- before_steps: the original steps (pass through as-is)
- after_steps: the Claude-augmented steps, marking which ones changed
- time_saved_estimate: realistic estimate (e.g. "~45 min per week per person")
- required_access: what the user needs to get started (e.g. Claude API access, specific integrations)
- starter_prompt: the key prompt to get started immediately
- success_signal: how the user knows it is working (observable outcome, not a feeling)
- common_mistakes: list of 2-3 mistakes to avoid"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=1000,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

playbook = generate_use_case_playbook(
    function="Legal",
    use_case_title="Contract Summary and Red-Flag Extraction",
    current_workflow_steps=[
        "Receive contract from counterparty via email",
        "Read full contract manually (30-90 pages)",
        "Highlight unusual or non-standard clauses",
        "Draft a summary memo for the business stakeholder",
        "Schedule review call with stakeholder"
    ],
    pain_points=[
        "Manual reading takes 2-4 hours per contract",
        "Summary memos lack consistency across different lawyers",
        "Business stakeholders often ask for the same clarifications each time"
    ]
)
print(f"Value: {playbook['executive_summary']}")
print(f"Time saved: {playbook['time_saved_estimate']}")
print(f"Success signal: {playbook['success_signal']}")

Champions are practitioners, not lawyers, security engineers, or ethicists. The escalation guide defines the decision tree that protects both the champion and the organisation when a colleague's request falls outside the champion's expertise:
Colleague wants to process regulated data (health, financial, HR records)
Output will be published externally (press release, regulatory filing)
Use case involves PII of individuals outside the organisation
Model behaviour seems unexpected or policy-violating
Champion → CoP council → Anthropic enterprise support
Request involves competitive intelligence or scraping third-party content

Publish the escalation guide as a one-page reference card that champions can share directly with colleagues. The goal is that the colleague always knows the next step — 'ask your champion' should never be the final answer.
A toolkit that goes stale faster than it is used destroys champion credibility. The update cadence must be realistic and owned:
— monthly review by two designated 'library curators' (senior champions); auto-flag prompts older than 6 months for re-validation after model updates
— updated within 5 business days of any policy, product, or regulatory change; champions can submit new questions via a shared form
— quarterly refresh; slides updated to reflect current Claude capabilities and the latest internal success stories
— authored reactively when a new validated use case emerges from the CoP showcase; deprecated when the workflow becomes a product feature (no longer needs a manual prompt)
— reviewed with legal and security at least annually; updated immediately on policy change

The champion enablement toolkit converts enthusiasm into repeatable, consistent impact. A curated prompt library reduces time-to-first-value from days to minutes. A structured lunch-and-learn kit gives champions a turn-key peer session with a live demo and hands-on segment. A policy-grounded FAQ playbook ensures consistent, accurate answers to the questions that arise in every adoption conversation. Use-case playbooks provide role-specific before/after workflows that convert sceptics. An escalation guide protects champions and colleagues when requests fall outside normal bounds. Claude can generate and validate each component — tailored criteria, agendas, FAQ responses, and playbooks — making the toolkit itself a demonstration of the capability it is designed to spread.
